Title: Glycinebetaine: an effective protectant against abiotic stress in plants

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: expression enhanced in roots after 24 h glycinebetaine treatment
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: expression enhanced in roots after 24 h glycinebetaine treatment
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 100 mM exogenous glycinebetaine; chilling at 4 °C for 24 h
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: DNA microarray; northern blot; knockout mutant analysis; chilling recovery assay
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. RabA4C expression was significantly induced by exogenous glycinebetaine in Arabidopsis roots. 2. Wild-type plants showed improved root growth after glycinebetaine pretreatment and chilling, whereas RabA4C knockout plants did not. 3. RabA4C is required for at least part of the protective effect of glycinebetaine on chilling tolerance.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: protects plants against abiotic stress including chilling
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This is a review article; RabA4C is summarized as a glycinebetaine-responsive downstream factor rather than a biosynthetic enzyme.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential marker or companion target for improving glycinebetaine-responsive chilling tolerance, though not presented as a primary engineering gene.

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: protein accumulation in response to exogenous glycinebetaine was concentration dependent
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: protein accumulation in response to exogenous glycinebetaine was concentration dependent
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: glycinebetaine treatment enhances accumulation of this cold-responsive protein associated with stress tolerance
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Exogenous glycinebetaine increased accumulation of WCOR410 protein in wheat. 2. The response was dependent on glycinebetaine concentration. 3. WCOR410 is implicated as a downstream component of glycinebetaine-induced cold tolerance.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: protects plants from abiotic stress, especially cold/freezing
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Downstream responsive gene, not a glycinebetaine biosynthetic gene.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: May be useful as a downstream marker of glycinebetaine responsiveness in cold tolerance breeding.

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: transcript accumulated after 250 mM exogenous glycinebetaine
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: transcript accumulated after 250 mM exogenous glycinebetaine
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: glycinebetaine induces transcript accumulation of a cold-responsive gene associated with stress tolerance
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Exogenous glycinebetaine induced accumulation of wcor413 transcripts in wheat. 2. This supports a role for glycinebetaine in activating specific cold-responsive genes. 3. The gene is summarized as a downstream component of glycinebetaine-mediated cold tolerance.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: protects plants from abiotic stress, especially cold/freezing
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Gene symbol reported in lowercase in the review text.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful as a marker of glycinebetaine-responsive cold-protection pathways.

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: transcript level elevated, strongest one day after chilling treatment following exogenous glycinebetaine application
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: transcript level elevated, strongest one day after chilling treatment following exogenous glycinebetaine application
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: glycinebetaine enhances cat1 expression and catalase activity during chilling stress
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Exogenous glycinebetaine increased catalase activity in tomato. 2. cat1 transcript abundance was elevated, with the strongest effect one day after chilling treatment. 3. cat1 likely contributes to glycinebetaine-associated suppression of oxidative damage during chilling.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: protects plants against chilling and oxidative stress
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Downstream antioxidant-response gene, not a core biosynthetic gene for glycinebetaine.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential supportive target for coupling glycinebetaine accumulation with antioxidant defense.

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: repressed in flowers of codA-transgenic plants but not in young leaves or flower buds
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: repressed in flowers of codA-transgenic plants but not in young leaves or flower buds
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: endogenous glycinebetaine accumulation in codA-transgenic plants; non-stress conditions
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: repression of fw2.2 is associated with glycinebetaine-linked enlargement of flowers and fruits
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. codA-transgenic tomato flower buds showed altered expression of multiple genes. 2. fw2.2, a negative regulator of cell division during early fruit development, was repressed in flowers. 3. This repression is linked to enlarged floral organs and heavier fruits in glycinebetaine-accumulating tomato plants.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: glycinebetaine accumulation is associated with improved reproductive development and yield traits
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This gene is a downstream developmental regulator affected by glycinebetaine accumulation, not a glycinebetaine metabolic gene.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: May be relevant when combining glycinebetaine engineering with fruit-size traits.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Arabidopsis; tomato; rice; potato; Brassica juncea; Diospyros kaki; Synechococcus sp. PCC 7942
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Lycopersicon esculentum; Oryza sativa; Solanum tuberosum; Brassica juncea; Diospyros kaki; Synechococcus sp. PCC 7942
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: leaves; flowers; siliques; inflorescence apices; chloroplasts
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: constitutive transgene expression in multiple plant organs; reproductive organs accumulated higher glycinebetaine than leaves in Arabidopsis
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: salt; chilling; freezing; heat; strong light; oxidative stress; drought; non-stress conditions
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic overexpression; subcellular targeting comparisons; metabolite profiling; stress tolerance assays; reproductive trait assessment; photosystem II analyses
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: upstream to terminal; direct synthesis route
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: oxidizes choline to glycinebetaine, via betaine aldehyde intermediate
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: chloroplasts; cytosol; chloroplasts + cytosol depending on construct
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: promotes glycinebetaine accumulation and enhances tolerance to multiple abiotic stresses; also improves reproductive performance and yield-related traits
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. codA is the most extensively used glycinebetaine-biosynthetic transgene summarized in the review. 2. codA expression increased glycinebetaine accumulation in multiple species and improved tolerance to salt, chilling, freezing, heat, strong light, oxidative stress, and drought. 3. Chloroplast-localized codA often conferred stronger protection than cytosolic localization despite lower total glycinebetaine. 4. In Arabidopsis and tomato, codA-associated glycinebetaine accumulation improved reproductive development and increased seeds, fruits, or flower size, including under non-stress conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review emphasizes that chloroplast localization can be more important than total glycinebetaine level. In rice, chloroplast-targeted codA protected photosynthetic machinery more efficiently than cytosol-targeted codA even with lower leaf glycinebetaine.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High-value engineering gene for broad abiotic stress tolerance and reproductive/yield protection; chloroplast targeting is recommended where photosynthetic protection is desired.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: compatible solute that protects against abiotic stress and supports yield stability
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No clear negative effect on Arabidopsis seed germination or early growth; generally positive or neutral under non-stress conditions

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Rice; Arabidopsis; Brassica napus; Nicotiana tabacum
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Oryza sativa; Arabidopsis thaliana; Brassica napus; Nicotiana tabacum
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: leaves; chloroplasts or cytosol depending on construct
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: transgene expression under ABA-inducible stress-inducible promoter or constitutive ubiquitin promoter in rice
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic overexpression; promoter comparison; metabolite quantification; stress tolerance assays
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: ABA-inducible stress-inducible promoter (SIP); ubiquitin constitutive promoter (UBI)
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: upstream to terminal; direct synthesis route
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: increases glycinebetaine accumulation and improves abiotic stress tolerance
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. cox transgenes from Arthrobacter were used to engineer glycinebetaine synthesis in several plants. 2. In rice, chloroplast-targeted cox under the constitutive ubiquitin promoter accumulated more glycinebetaine than under an ABA-inducible promoter. 3. cox-expressing plants exhibited enhanced salt, drought, and/or freezing tolerance depending on species.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review notes that the ABA-inducible promoter was not particularly effective for de novo glycinebetaine production compared with constitutive UBI in rice.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful alternative to codA for engineering glycinebetaine synthesis; constitutive strong promoters may outperform inducible promoters for metabolite accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: compatible solute protecting against abiotic stress
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: ABA-inducible expression gave lower glycinebetaine accumulation than constitutive expression in rice

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Zea mays; Nicotiana tabacum; Gossypium hirsutum; Oryza sativa
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: leaves; mainly cytosol in summarized examples; mitochondria in modified rice construct
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: transgenic expression in leaves and reproductive tissues inferred from stress phenotypes
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic overexpression; metabolite profiling; field drought tests; stress tolerance phenotyping
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: oxidizes or dehydrogenates choline to betaine aldehyde
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: cytosol in most plant examples summarized; mitochondria in modified rice construct
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: elevates glycinebetaine biosynthesis and improves drought, chilling, and salt tolerance; in maize improves reproductive performance and grain yield under drought
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. betA-transgenic maize accumulated substantially more glycinebetaine in leaves and showed improved chilling and drought tolerance. 2. Under field drought, transgenic maize had less inhibited reproductive development and 10–23% higher grain weight per plant. 3. In maize, enhanced drought tolerance was associated mainly with cytosolic glycinebetaine because chloroplastic glycinebetaine remained low. 4. betA was also used successfully in tobacco, cotton, and modified rice.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Because betA produces betaine aldehyde, effective glycinebetaine accumulation in plants implies endogenous or co-functioning BADH activity downstream.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for improving drought resilience and reproductive-stage stability in crops such as maize.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: compatible solute protecting photosynthesis, growth, and reproductive development under stress

Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic overexpression; metabolite quantification; chloroplast localization analysis; PSII photoinhibition/repair assays
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: supports glycinebetaine accumulation and improves heat tolerance, including protection of PSII repair
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BADH from spinach enabled glycinebetaine accumulation in chloroplast-targeted transgenic tobacco. 2. Most glycinebetaine accumulated in chloroplasts in these plants. 3. Glycinebetaine accumulation increased resistance of PSII to moderate-heat-stress-stimulated photoinhibition by enhancing PSII repair.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: In Arabidopsis, BADH was also summarized together with CMO as a two-gene engineering strategy for salt tolerance.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful terminal-step gene for pathway reconstruction and chloroplast-focused engineering of glycinebetaine-mediated thermotolerance.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: compatible solute protecting photosynthetic machinery and stress tolerance

Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic overexpression; metabolite profiling; stress tolerance assays
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: upstream; first committed oxidation step in plant pathway
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: converts choline to betaine aldehyde through monooxygenation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: enables glycinebetaine biosynthesis and improves stress tolerance when introduced into plants
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CMO is one of the plant glycinebetaine-biosynthetic enzymes summarized in the review. 2. Chloroplast-targeted CMO in rice led to glycinebetaine accumulation and enhanced salt and low day/night temperature tolerance. 3. Combined CMO and BADH expression in Arabidopsis also supported strong glycinebetaine accumulation and salt tolerance.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Presented in the review as part of engineered pathway reconstruction, not as the dominant transgene compared with codA.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for reconstructing a plant-like glycinebetaine pathway, especially in combination with BADH.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: compatible solute for abiotic stress protection

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: transgenic cytosolic expression; glycine supplementation used in one experiment
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic overexpression with ApDMT; metabolite profiling; stress tolerance assays
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: catalyzes the first two methylation steps converting glycine to sarcosine and sarcosine to dimethylglycine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: supports de novo glycinebetaine synthesis via glycine methylation and enhances salt/chilling tolerance in transgenic Arabidopsis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ApGSMT from the halotolerant cyanobacterium Aphanothece halophytica was used with ApDMT to engineer glycinebetaine synthesis in Arabidopsis. 2. The pathway uses glycine methylation rather than choline oxidation. 3. The review states that these methylation enzymes showed no apparent advantage over choline-oxidizing enzymes for engineering glycinebetaine biosynthesis in plants.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review explicitly notes no apparent advantages of ApGSMT/ApDMT over choline-oxidizing enzymes in plant engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Alternative pathway option where choline supply is limiting, but not evidently superior to choline-oxidation genes.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: compatible solute conferring abiotic stress protection
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No apparent engineering advantage over choline-oxidizing enzymes

Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic overexpression with ApGSMT; metabolite profiling; stress tolerance assays
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: completes glycinebetaine biosynthesis via the glycine methylation pathway and contributes to enhanced abiotic stress tolerance
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ApDMT catalyzes the final methylation of dimethylglycine to glycinebetaine in the Aphanothece pathway. 2. In combination with ApGSMT, it enabled glycinebetaine production in transgenic Arabidopsis. 3. The engineered plants exhibited enhanced salt and chilling tolerance, but the pathway had no clear advantage over choline-oxidation routes.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Discussed mainly in Box 1 and in the comparison of engineering strategies.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful as part of an alternative glycine-to-glycinebetaine engineering module, especially together with ApGSMT.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: compatible solute conferring abiotic stress protection
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No apparent advantage over choline-oxidizing enzymes
